APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper proposes evaluation metrics (prerequisite skill taxonomy and readability features) and applies them to six standard RC datasets (SQuAD, MCTest, QA4MRE, MS MARCO, NewsQA), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues concerned with dataset analysis and evaluation. The methodology (manual annotation of required sentences/skills, use of established readability features from Vajjala & Meurers, and empirical analysis of dataset properties) matches community standards for dataset/benchmark papers. The work is targeted to the NLP/ML audience that builds and evaluates reading-comprehension systems and cites relevant prior RC and evaluation literature, so it is well positioned for such venues. | B(5): Yes — this paper addresses dataset evaluation for reading comprehension, a core NLP problem highly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes two classes of evaluation metrics ("prerequisite skills" and "readability") grounded in cognitive models (McNamara & Magliano) and refines/annotates 13 skills (Section 3.1), then annotates and analyzes six major RC datasets including SQuAD and MCTest (Section 4 and Table 6). The methodology (manual annotation, use of Vajjala & Meurers readability features, and cross-dataset analysis) and positioning among RC and textual-entailment literature make it well suited to the venue’s audience and standards.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes evaluation metrics (prerequisite skill taxonomy and readability features) and applies them to six standard RC datasets (SQuAD, MCTest, QA4MRE, MS MARCO, NewsQA), which is squarely within the scope of top NLP venues concerned with dataset analysis and evaluation. The methodology (manual annotation of required sentences/skills, use of established readability features from Vajjala & Meurers, and empirical analysis of dataset properties) matches community standards for dataset/benchmark papers. The work is targeted to the NLP/ML audience that builds and evaluates reading-comprehension systems and cites relevant prior RC and evaluation literature, so it is well positioned for such venues.

reviewer_b.justification: Yes — this paper addresses dataset evaluation for reading comprehension, a core NLP problem highly relevant to top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes two classes of evaluation metrics ("prerequisite skills" and "readability") grounded in cognitive models (McNamara & Magliano) and refines/annotates 13 skills (Section 3.1), then annotates and analyzes six major RC datasets including SQuAD and MCTest (Section 4 and Table 6). The methodology (manual annotation, use of Vajjala & Meurers readability features, and cross-dataset analysis) and positioning among RC and textual-entailment literature make it well suited to the venue’s audience and standards.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a systematic, empirically grounded evaluation framework by combining two classes of metrics (prerequisite skills and readability) and refining Sugawara et al.’s schema into 13 skills (“By refining Sugawara et al. (2017)’s ten reading comprehension skills, we reorganized thirteen prerequisite skills...”) and annotating six RC datasets. However, the core components are drawn from existing theories and resources (McNamara & Magliano’s psychology model, Vajjala & Meurers’ readability features) and mostly extend/operationalize prior work rather than introducing a fundamentally new idea. Overall this is a useful, moderate-novelty contribution—systematic and broader in scope than earlier two-dataset analyses but not a major methodological breakthrough. | B(3): The paper provides a useful, systematic refinement and combination of existing ideas rather than a wholly new paradigm: it refines Sugawara et al.’s ten skills into thirteen prerequisite skills (Section 3.1), adopts McNamara & Magliano’s two-class psychology framing for metrics, and reuses Vajjala & Meurers’ readability features. It also applies these combined metrics to annotate six RC datasets (Section 4), which is a non-trivial empirical contribution but primarily an extension and organization of prior work. Overall this is an incremental contribution with moderate novelty rather than a fundamentally new method.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a systematic, empirically grounded evaluation framework by combining two classes of metrics (prerequisite skills and readability) and refining Sugawara et al.’s schema into 13 skills (“By refining Sugawara et al. (2017)’s ten reading comprehension skills, we reorganized thirteen prerequisite skills...”) and annotating six RC datasets. However, the core components are drawn from existing theories and resources (McNamara & Magliano’s psychology model, Vajjala & Meurers’ readability features) and mostly extend/operationalize prior work rather than introducing a fundamentally new idea. Overall this is a useful, moderate-novelty contribution—systematic and broader in scope than earlier two-dataset analyses but not a major methodological breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a useful, systematic refinement and combination of existing ideas rather than a wholly new paradigm: it refines Sugawara et al.’s ten skills into thirteen prerequisite skills (Section 3.1), adopts McNamara & Magliano’s two-class psychology framing for metrics, and reuses Vajjala & Meurers’ readability features. It also applies these combined metrics to annotate six RC datasets (Section 4), which is a non-trivial empirical contribution but primarily an extension and organization of prior work. Overall this is an incremental contribution with moderate novelty rather than a fundamentally new method.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—measuring RC dataset difficulty—by proposing two evaluation classes (prerequisite skills and readability), refining a 13-skill taxonomy, and annotating six existing datasets (Section 3.1 and 4). Its finding that readability and question difficulty are not strongly linked and the suggested use of metrics for curriculum-style dataset construction (Section 6) provide useful guidance that could change how dataset designers and RC researchers assess and construct benchmarks. While not transformational, the work offers a broadly applicable, empirical framework likely to be adopted for dataset analysis and future dataset/system development. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical problem—systematic evaluation of RC datasets—by proposing two metric classes grounded in psychology (prerequisite skills, refined to 13 categories in Section 3.1, and readability features from Vajjala & Meurers) and by annotating six existing RC datasets (Section 4). Its empirical finding that readability does not directly determine question difficulty and that ‘‘easy-to-read but difficult-to-answer’’ datasets can be constructed (Section 6) can change how dataset creators and researchers design benchmarks and curricula (the authors explicitly discuss curriculum and transfer learning). These results are likely to be adopted by dataset builders and spur follow-up work on dataset diagnostics and curriculum design, though the contribution is not a sweeping theoretical breakthrough.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—measuring RC dataset difficulty—by proposing two evaluation classes (prerequisite skills and readability), refining a 13-skill taxonomy, and annotating six existing datasets (Section 3.1 and 4). Its finding that readability and question difficulty are not strongly linked and the suggested use of metrics for curriculum-style dataset construction (Section 6) provide useful guidance that could change how dataset designers and RC researchers assess and construct benchmarks. While not transformational, the work offers a broadly applicable, empirical framework likely to be adopted for dataset analysis and future dataset/system development.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—systematic evaluation of RC datasets—by proposing two metric classes grounded in psychology (prerequisite skills, refined to 13 categories in Section 3.1, and readability features from Vajjala & Meurers) and by annotating six existing RC datasets (Section 4). Its empirical finding that readability does not directly determine question difficulty and that ‘‘easy-to-read but difficult-to-answer’’ datasets can be constructed (Section 6) can change how dataset creators and researchers design benchmarks and curricula (the authors explicitly discuss curriculum and transfer learning). These results are likely to be adopted by dataset builders and spur follow-up work on dataset diagnostics and curriculum design, though the contribution is not a sweeping theoretical breakthrough.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper carries out a reasonably broad empirical analysis by annotating six RC datasets with a refined set of 13 prerequisite skills and measuring sentence counts/distances (e.g., Table 6) and readability using Vajjala & Meurers (2012) features, and it discusses annotation issues and dataset limitations (e.g., disagreement about "nonsense" questions, inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence). However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses, does not report key reproducibility details such as annotation counts, inter-annotator agreement, or exact sampling sizes, and provides little methodological detail (labeling guidelines, code/configs) to fully reproduce results. Related work coverage and explicit discussion of limitations are strong, but experimental depth and reproducibility are incomplete. | B(3): The paper applies the proposed metrics to six RC datasets and reports dataset-level analyses (e.g., average number and distance of required sentences; Table 6), which supports some claims about dataset characteristics, but key experimental details (sample sizes, inter-annotator agreement, exact feature/metric computations) are missing, limiting reproducibility. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are described. Related work appears broad and well integrated (psychology and NLP literatures are cited), and several limitations are explicitly discussed (annotation disagreements, inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence), but the empirical evaluation lacks some important analyses and reporting necessary for full validation.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper carries out a reasonably broad empirical analysis by annotating six RC datasets with a refined set of 13 prerequisite skills and measuring sentence counts/distances (e.g., Table 6) and readability using Vajjala & Meurers (2012) features, and it discusses annotation issues and dataset limitations (e.g., disagreement about "nonsense" questions, inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence). However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses, does not report key reproducibility details such as annotation counts, inter-annotator agreement, or exact sampling sizes, and provides little methodological detail (labeling guidelines, code/configs) to fully reproduce results. Related work coverage and explicit discussion of limitations are strong, but experimental depth and reproducibility are incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies the proposed metrics to six RC datasets and reports dataset-level analyses (e.g., average number and distance of required sentences; Table 6), which supports some claims about dataset characteristics, but key experimental details (sample sizes, inter-annotator agreement, exact feature/metric computations) are missing, limiting reproducibility. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are described. Related work appears broad and well integrated (psychology and NLP literatures are cited), and several limitations are explicitly discussed (annotation disagreements, inability to evaluate sentence-selection competence), but the empirical evaluation lacks some important analyses and reporting necessary for full validation.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper has a generally logical structure (related work, metrics, annotation, discussion) and the methodology and experiments are presented, but the supplied text contains substantial repetition and duplicated paragraphs (e.g., multiple repeats of “Our two classes of metrics…” and blocks listing the prerequisite skills) which hampers readability. The main contributions are described but not crisply stated up front, and there are a few formatting/clarity issues with figures/tables (Table 6 is present but context and caption are fragmented). Notation is minimal and mostly consistent, but the writing suffers from occasional grammatical slips and ambiguous phrasing (e.g., incomplete sentences and abrupt transitions) that make some sections hard to follow. | B(3): The paper has an overall logical structure (related work -> metrics -> annotation -> discussion) and presents a clear numbered list of prerequisite skills (Section 3.1), but presentation suffers from frequent repetition and editing artifacts that impede flow (e.g., many duplicated sentences such as “Based on McNamara and Magliano (2009)’s depiction...” and repeated paragraphs). Figures/tables are present but poorly formatted in the excerpt (Table 6 is garbled and Figure 1 is referenced without clear placement), and there are LaTeX/footnote artifacts scattered through the text. Notation is minimal and the skills list is consistent, yet the writing contains awkward phrasing and formatting errors that require careful proofreading and cleanup.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper has a generally logical structure (related work, metrics, annotation, discussion) and the methodology and experiments are presented, but the supplied text contains substantial repetition and duplicated paragraphs (e.g., multiple repeats of “Our two classes of metrics…” and blocks listing the prerequisite skills) which hampers readability. The main contributions are described but not crisply stated up front, and there are a few formatting/clarity issues with figures/tables (Table 6 is present but context and caption are fragmented). Notation is minimal and mostly consistent, but the writing suffers from occasional grammatical slips and ambiguous phrasing (e.g., incomplete sentences and abrupt transitions) that make some sections hard to follow.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper has an overall logical structure (related work -> metrics -> annotation -> discussion) and presents a clear numbered list of prerequisite skills (Section 3.1), but presentation suffers from frequent repetition and editing artifacts that impede flow (e.g., many duplicated sentences such as “Based on McNamara and Magliano (2009)’s depiction...” and repeated paragraphs). Figures/tables are present but poorly formatted in the excerpt (Table 6 is garbled and Figure 1 is referenced without clear placement), and there are LaTeX/footnote artifacts scattered through the text. Notation is minimal and the skills list is consistent, yet the writing contains awkward phrasing and formatting errors that require careful proofreading and cleanup.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper defines reasonable metrics (prerequisite skills in Section 3.1 and readability using Vajjala & Meurers features) and applies them to six datasets (annotation procedure in Section 4.1 and Table 6), but key methodological details and evidence are missing. The annotation process reports three annotators yet provides no inter-annotator agreement, sample sizes per dataset, or statistical tests to support claims (Section 4.1 and Discussion), and the sentence-distance measure (Appendix B) is ambiguously defined. The core assumption that the number of required skills corresponds to question difficulty is asserted (citing Sugawara et al.) but not empirically validated in the provided text, making the conclusion that readability does not directly affect question difficulty under-supported. Overall the approach is plausible but some important experimental controls and clarity are lacking. | B(3): The paper clearly states its main claims (two metric classes, a refined 13-skill taxonomy, and dataset analyses) and presents experimental annotations and summary statistics (e.g., Table 6). However, methodological details necessary to support those claims are missing or unclear: no inter-annotator agreement or sample sizes are reported, the sentence-distance measure is vaguely defined (“sum of distances of any two sentences”), and no statistical tests are given to support claims (e.g., that readability does not affect question difficulty). There are acknowledged annotation limitations (nonsense/no-answer handling) and some unsupported assumptions (equating number of skills to question difficulty) that weaken the experimental support for the conclusions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper defines reasonable metrics (prerequisite skills in Section 3.1 and readability using Vajjala & Meurers features) and applies them to six datasets (annotation procedure in Section 4.1 and Table 6), but key methodological details and evidence are missing. The annotation process reports three annotators yet provides no inter-annotator agreement, sample sizes per dataset, or statistical tests to support claims (Section 4.1 and Discussion), and the sentence-distance measure (Appendix B) is ambiguously defined. The core assumption that the number of required skills corresponds to question difficulty is asserted (citing Sugawara et al.) but not empirically validated in the provided text, making the conclusion that readability does not directly affect question difficulty under-supported. Overall the approach is plausible but some important experimental controls and clarity are lacking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states its main claims (two metric classes, a refined 13-skill taxonomy, and dataset analyses) and presents experimental annotations and summary statistics (e.g., Table 6). However, methodological details necessary to support those claims are missing or unclear: no inter-annotator agreement or sample sizes are reported, the sentence-distance measure is vaguely defined (“sum of distances of any two sentences”), and no statistical tests are given to support claims (e.g., that readability does not affect question difficulty). There are acknowledged annotation limitations (nonsense/no-answer handling) and some unsupported assumptions (equating number of skills to question difficulty) that weaken the experimental support for the conclusions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper situates its metrics against relevant prior work (e.g., refining Sugawara et al. (2017)’s skills, using Vajjala & Meurers (2012) for readability features, and discussing Kintsch/McNamara distinctions) and annotates six RC datasets for comparative analysis (Table 6). It explicitly acknowledges limitations of prior methods (e.g., Pitler & Nenkova (2008) unsuitable here, CNN/Daily Mail issues from Chen et al. (2016)) and explains methodological differences (new/modified skill categories). However, it does not present a quantitative head-to-head comparison of its proposed evaluation metrics against other existing dataset-evaluation frameworks, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive. | B(3): The paper cites and builds on relevant prior work (e.g., it adopts and refines Sugawara et al. (2017)’s prerequisite skills and uses Vajjala and Meurers (2012) for readability features) and applies the metrics to six datasets including SQuAD and MCTest (Sections 3.1, 4, and dataset analyses). However, it does not present explicit empirical comparisons against alternative evaluation frameworks or baselines for dataset difficulty (it discusses prior work such as Pitler & Nenkova (2008) and Chen et al. (2016) but does not quantitatively compare methods), nor does it acknowledge cases where prior metrics might perform comparably or better. Thus comparisons to prior approaches are present in discussion/citation but lack comprehensive, quantitative baseline benchmarking.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper situates its metrics against relevant prior work (e.g., refining Sugawara et al. (2017)’s skills, using Vajjala & Meurers (2012) for readability features, and discussing Kintsch/McNamara distinctions) and annotates six RC datasets for comparative analysis (Table 6). It explicitly acknowledges limitations of prior methods (e.g., Pitler & Nenkova (2008) unsuitable here, CNN/Daily Mail issues from Chen et al. (2016)) and explains methodological differences (new/modified skill categories). However, it does not present a quantitative head-to-head comparison of its proposed evaluation metrics against other existing dataset-evaluation frameworks, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper cites and builds on relevant prior work (e.g., it adopts and refines Sugawara et al. (2017)’s prerequisite skills and uses Vajjala and Meurers (2012) for readability features) and applies the metrics to six datasets including SQuAD and MCTest (Sections 3.1, 4, and dataset analyses). However, it does not present explicit empirical comparisons against alternative evaluation frameworks or baselines for dataset difficulty (it discusses prior work such as Pitler & Nenkova (2008) and Chen et al. (2016) but does not quantitatively compare methods), nor does it acknowledge cases where prior metrics might perform comparably or better. Thus comparisons to prior approaches are present in discussion/citation but lack comprehensive, quantitative baseline benchmarking.
